children.value: The TAMBI task generates rich trial-by-trial behavioral data (32-slot net allocations over ~180 trials). To connect this behavior to latent cognitive processes, we require a generative model that:
Retains the scientific structure of the task (2-state HMM, cue offset, oddball mixture, von-Mises observation model)
Has parameters that directly and transparently affect behavioral output
Produces behavioral patterns that match the range observed in human participants
Is computationally efficient enough to generate the ~100,000 simulations required for amortized inference training

The reduced-form agent satisfies all four requirements. It replaces the particle filter approximation (used in the simulation models for model comparison) with analytically tractable update rules, ensuring that each parameter has a direct, measurable effect on the allocation output without being “washed out” by particle dynamics. Additionally, the simplified action selection allows to focus on the underlying process of inference and belief updating - implementing inference with free parameters for both at a time would cause critical ambiguity and uncontrolable parameter-interaction effects in the inference process.
The agent maintains point estimates of the following latent state variables, updated on each trial:

children.html: μA\mu_AμA​

children.value: Believed attack direction for Cue A under the current z-state

children.html: δB\delta_BδB​

children.value: Angular offset between Cue A and Cue B directions

children.html: Δ\DeltaΔ
p(zt=1)p(z_t = 1)p(zt​=1)

Two structural parameters are fixed across all participants:

children.html: κfixed\kappa_{\text{fixed}}κfixed​

children.value: Calibrated so that precision_weighting produces the full range of VP-observed allocation patterns (1–7 occupied slots, with realistic stacking)

children.html: ρfixed\rho_{\text{fixed}}ρfixed​

children.value: Baseline oddball probability matching the TAMBI design (~10% oddball rate)

children.value: . This prevents a confound where precision weighting would simultaneously make the agent more confident in its predictions
The agent has four free parameters estimated per participant via BayesFlow:

children.html: pw∈[0.1,5.0]\text{pw} \in [0.1, 5.0]pw∈[0.1,5.0]

children.value: Controls how tightly the net allocation follows the agent’s learned direction. Mechanically, precision weighting controls three aspects of the agent’s behavior:

children.html: κeff=κfixed⋅pw\kappa_{\text{eff}} = \kappa_{\text{fixed}} \cdot \text{pw}κeff​=κfixed​⋅pw

children.value: This scales the von Mises concentration used in the predictive distribution, determining how sharply the predicted attack distribution peaks around the believed direction.

children.html: α=1+pw\alpha = 1 + \text{pw}α=1+pw
πs\pi_sπs​
α\alphaα

children.value: before integer rounding, controlling how concentrated the net placement is.

children.html: ημ=0.05⋅pw\eta_\mu = 0.05 \cdot \text{pw}ημ​=0.05⋅pw

children.value: This scales the prediction-error-driven update of

children.html: μ^A\hat{\mu}_Aμ^​A​

children.value: , determining how quickly the agent adjusts its direction estimate after each observation.

These three mechanisms are intentionally coupled to represent a certain mechanistic behavioral pattern. An agent with high precision weighting believes outcomes are precise (narrow predictive distribution), places nets precisely (high sharpening), and updates its direction estimate quickly (high learning rate). This coherence reflects the theoretical construct of precision weighting in predictive processing (Friston, 2008; Mathys et al., 2014).

children.value: Controls how the agent attributes surprising observations to noise (oddballs) versus genuine environmental change (state switches). (This is inspired by the computational formalization of the Piray & Daw (2021) “broken unpredictability estimator” hypothesis)

Additionally, noise reactivity gates the direction update via the oddball responsibility:

children.value: . That means, when noise reactivity is high,

children.html: ρeff\rho_{\text{eff}}ρeff​
γ\gammaγ

children.value: is high for surprising observations, and the update is dampened (the agent treats the surprise as noise and shows weaker learning from this trial). When noise reactivity is low,

children.html: ρeff\rho_{\text{eff}}ρeff​
γ\gammaγ

children.value: is low, and the agent fully updates its direction estimate (treating the surprise as a real signal). Low noise reactivity (

children.html: nr<1\text{nr} < 1nr<1

children.value: ) corresponds to an agent that underestimates the oddball rate, causing unexpected outcomes to be interpreted as state changes rather than random fluctuations, which produces a behavioral pattern that is characterized by excessive belief updating, abrupt state switches, and elevated perceived volatility. The complementary case (high noise reactivity (

children.html: nr>1\text{nr} > 1nr>1

children.value: )) describes an agent that over-attributes surprising outcomes to noise, potentially missing genuine state changes.
(My idea was that this parameter could seperate anxiety and depression in a reasonable manner)

children.html: bm=0.9\text{bm} = 0.9bm=0.9

children.value: , only 10% of the new evidence is incorporated per trial, requiring ~10 trials to approach the new posterior. This parameter captures individual differences in cognitive flexibility versus rigidity, and may relate to intolerance of uncertainty or perseverative tendencies. It is distinct from noise reactivity in that it applies to
belief updates (including those following genuine state changes), not only to oddball attribution.

These latent features are used during training to provide the neural network with rich temporal information about the agent’s internal dynamics. For participant data inference, a proxy forward pass with default parameters generates these features (see Section xxx).
Friston, K. (2008). Hierarchical models in the brain.

Mathys, C. D., Lomakina, E. I., Daunizeau, J., Iglesias, S., Brodersen, K. H., Friston, K. J., & Stephan, K. E. (2014). Uncertainty in perception and the Hierarchical Gaussian Filter. Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 8, 825.
